name: Mountain Mahogany
description: Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus spp.) is a shrub or small tree in the rose family that is toxic to cats due to cyanogenic glycosides — primarily prunasin — found in its leaves. When the leaves are chewed or wilted, prunasin is enzymatically converted to hydrogen cyanide (HCN), which blocks cellular respiration. Wilted or dried leaves are particularly dangerous as the enzymatic conversion proceeds more readily.
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: prunasin (cyanogenic glycoside); wilted leaves most dangerous
Mechanism: prunasin releases HCN via beta-glucosidase, inhibiting cytochrome c oxidase and halting cellular respiration
Symptoms: rapid breathing, weakness, dilated pupils, drooling; severe cases cause seizures, coma, and respiratory failure
Wilted or fallen leaves pose a higher risk than fresh leaves due to accelerated cyanogenic conversion
Seek veterinary care immediately if ingestion is suspected — cyanide toxicity can progress within minutes

symptoms.name: Respiratory distress (panting, gasping for breath, or labored breathing)
symptoms.notes: This is typically one of the most prominent signs of cyanide poisoning, occurring because the toxin severely hampers the body's ability to utilize oxygen at the cellular level45. 2. Unusual Gum Color (Bright Red Gums)

symptoms.name: Unusually red or pink gums (bright red mucous membranes)
symptoms.notes: This distinct symptom happens because the cells are unable to extract oxygen from the blood. The blood remains fully oxygenated, causing the gums and mucous membranes to appear bright red while the body's tissues are essentially suffocating35. 3. Dilated Pupils

symptoms.name: Dilated pupils
symptoms.notes: Abnormally large pupils act as an indicator of severe systemic stress and neurological impairment from the rapid lack of usable oxygen3.... 4. Excessive Drooling

symptoms.name: Excessive drooling (hypersalivation)
symptoms.notes: Salivating much more than usual can be one of the earlier, acute signs of toxic exposure before severe neurological decline sets in5. 5. Shock, Seizures, and Coma

symptoms.name: Shock, seizures (convulsions), loss of consciousness, and coma
symptoms.notes: These extreme neurological and cardiovascular symptoms manifest as the oxygen deprivation peaks. Cyanide poisoning acts very quickly; if prompt veterinary attention (which may include oxygen therapy and specific antidotes) is not provided, it rapidly progresses to death
